docs.title: genoselect
docs.text:
scikit-learn-compatible genomic prediction and selection from SNP marker data.

Genomic selection is dominated by R packages (rrBLUP, BGLR, sommer). genoselect brings a clean, Pythonic, scikit-learn-compatible toolkit to the same problem: fit, cross-validate, and benchmark genomic-prediction models from 0/1/2 marker matrices, with a properly implemented GBLUP by REML at its core — validated against rrBLUP to ~1e-6.

docs.title: Install
docs.text: pip install genoselect

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
import genoselect as gs

pop = gs.simulate_population(n=250, m=800, n_qtl=40, h2=0.5, seed=1)

fit = gs.GBLUP().fit(pop.geno, pop.pheno)   # GBLUP by REML
print(fit.h2_)                              # genomic heritability
pred = fit.predict(pop.geno[:10])           # predict new genotypes

cv = gs.benchmark(pop.geno, pop.pheno, k=5, random_state=1)
print(cv.summary())                         # compare models

Load your own data:

gd = gs.read_vcf("genotypes.vcf.gz")   # or read_plink(...), read_hapmap(...)
fit = gs.GBLUP().fit(gd.geno, phenotypes)

docs.title: Where to go next
docs.text:
• Tutorial — a full worked example with a model benchmark and the rrBLUP validation.
  • User guide — installation, data conventions, and every function.
  • API reference — auto-generated from the source.
  • PDF guide — the user guide as a printable PDF.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• VanRaden genomic relationship matrix
  • GBLUP by REML (Endelman spectral method) + RR-BLUP marker effects
  • A common interface to GBLUP, elastic net, random forest, gradient boosting, and a stacked super-learner ensemble
  • Breeding-relevant cross-validation (k-fold, leave-group-out)
  • One-line readers for VCF / PLINK / HapMap
  • Population simulation and quality control

Released under the MIT License · GitHub

docs.title: genoselect.GBLUP
docs.text:
Bases: BaseEstimator, RegressorMixin

Genomic BLUP regressor.

Fits GBLUP on a 0/1/2 marker matrix, estimating variance components by REML. The equivalent ridge-regression marker effects are derived so the model can predict breeding values for unseen genotypes.

Attributes (set after fit)

h2_ : float Estimated genomic heritability Vu / (Vu + Ve). Vu_, Ve_, lambda_ : float Genetic and residual variances and their ratio. gebv_ : ndarray Genomic estimated breeding values for the training individuals. marker_effects_ : ndarray RR-BLUP marker effects (for out-of-sample prediction).

docs.title: genoselect.StackedEnsemble
docs.text:
Bases: BaseEstimator, RegressorMixin

Super-learner that combines base models with non-negative weights.

Out-of-fold predictions from an inner cross-validation are stacked, and the combination weights are found by non-negative least squares (then normalised to sum to one), following the super-learner of van der Laan et al. (2007). Base models are then refit on the full training data.

docs.title: genoselect.available_models()
docs.text: Names of the genomic-prediction models genoselect can run.

docs.title: genoselect.make_model(name, random_state=None)
docs.text:
Construct a fresh, unfitted estimator by name.

All estimators follow the scikit-learn fit(X, y) / predict(X) API, where X is a 0/1/2 marker matrix.

docs.title: genoselect.gblup
docs.text: GBLUP by REML (Endelman 2011 spectral method), scikit-learn compatible.

docs.title: GBLUP
docs.text:
Bases: BaseEstimator, RegressorMixin

Genomic BLUP regressor.

Fits GBLUP on a 0/1/2 marker matrix, estimating variance components by REML. The equivalent ridge-regression marker effects are derived so the model can predict breeding values for unseen genotypes.

Attributes (set after fit)

h2_ : float Estimated genomic heritability Vu / (Vu + Ve). Vu_, Ve_, lambda_ : float Genetic and residual variances and their ratio. gebv_ : ndarray Genomic estimated breeding values for the training individuals. marker_effects_ : ndarray RR-BLUP marker effects (for out-of-sample prediction).

docs.title: reml_solve(y, K, X=None)
docs.text:
Solve the mixed model y = Xb + g + e, g ~ N(0, K s2u), e ~ N(0, I s2e).

Variance components are estimated by REML using the spectral approach of Endelman (2011) — the profiled restricted log-likelihood is minimised over the single ratio lambda = s2e / s2u. Returns a dict with Vu, Ve, h2, beta, gebv, lambda, Hinv and the residuals (y - Xb).

docs.title: gblup(y, geno=None, K=None, X=None, min_maf=0.0)
docs.text:
Functional interface mirroring the R GSbench::gblup.

With geno it returns a fitted :class:GBLUP (which can predict new genotypes); with only K it returns the REML solution dict (no out-of-sample prediction).

docs.title: genoselect.reml_solve(y, K, X=None)
docs.text:
Solve the mixed model y = Xb + g + e, g ~ N(0, K s2u), e ~ N(0, I s2e).

Variance components are estimated by REML using the spectral approach of Endelman (2011) — the profiled restricted log-likelihood is minimised over the single ratio lambda = s2e / s2u. Returns a dict with Vu, Ve, h2, beta, gebv, lambda, Hinv and the residuals (y - Xb).

docs.title: genoselect.vanraden_grm(geno, min_maf=0.0)
docs.text:
VanRaden (2008) genomic relationship matrix from 0/1/2 dosages.

G = W Wᵀ / (2 Σ p(1-p)), with W = M - 2p the centred marker matrix.

docs.title: genoselect.centre_markers(geno, min_maf=0.0)
docs.text:
Centre markers by 2p and return the VanRaden scaling.

Returns (W, p, c, keep) where W is the centred marker matrix (n x m_kept), p the allele frequencies of the kept markers, c the VanRaden normaliser 2 * sum p(1-p), and keep the boolean column mask.

docs.title: genoselect.cross_validate(geno, y, models=None, k=5, reps=1, scheme='kfold', groups=None, random_state=None)
docs.text:
Cross-validate genomic-prediction models.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default geno array (n x m)

Marker matrix coded 0/1/2.

required y array(n)

Phenotypes.

required models str | list[str] | None

Model name(s); defaults to ["gblup"]. See :func:available_models.

None k int

Number of folds for scheme="kfold".

5 scheme ('kfold', 'leave_group_out')

leave_group_out requires groups (e.g. families/environments).

"kfold"

docs.title: genoselect.benchmark
docs.text: Benchmark several genomic-prediction models on the same data.

docs.title: benchmark(geno, y, models=None, k=5, reps=1, scheme='kfold', groups=None, random_state=None)
docs.text:
Cross-validate all (or selected) models and return a comparable summary.

Defaults to every model except the (slow, nested) ensemble. Use CVResult.summary() for a tidy table sorted by predictive ability.

docs.title: genoselect.CVResult
docs.text: Per-fold predictive abilities for one or more models.

docs.title: genoselect.gwas
docs.text: Single-marker genome-wide association (GWAS) with structure correction.

docs.title: gwas(geno, y, marker_map=None, n_pc=0, covariates=None)
docs.text:
Single-marker additive association scan.

Each marker is tested for association with y after regressing out an intercept, optional covariates, and (optionally) the first n_pc genotype principal components to correct for population structure.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default geno array(n, m)

Marker matrix coded 0/1/2.

required y array(n)

Phenotype (missing values are dropped).

required marker_map DataFrame or dict

Per-marker annotation; recognised columns marker, chrom, pos are carried through to the result (enabling position-aware Manhattan plots).

None n_pc int

Number of genotype PCs to include as covariates.

0 covariates array(n, k)

Extra fixed covariates.

None

Returns:

Type Description DataFrame

One row per marker with columns index [, marker, chrom, pos], effect, se, t, p and log10p.

docs.title: manhattan(result, alpha=0.05, ax=None, colors=('#2c7fb8', '#8fbcd4'), title='GWAS Manhattan')
docs.text: Manhattan plot from a :func:gwas result (position-aware if chrom).

docs.title: qq_plot(result, ax=None, title='QQ plot')
docs.text: QQ plot of observed vs expected -log10(p) from a :func:gwas result.

docs.title: genoselect.manhattan(result, alpha=0.05, ax=None, colors=('#2c7fb8', '#8fbcd4'), title='GWAS Manhattan')
docs.text: Manhattan plot from a :func:gwas result (position-aware if chrom).

docs.title: genoselect.qq_plot(result, ax=None, title='QQ plot')
docs.text: QQ plot of observed vs expected -log10(p) from a :func:gwas result.

docs.title: genoselect.read_vcf(path)
docs.text:
Read a (optionally gzipped) VCF into dosages = number of ALT alleles.

Biallelic diploid sites are used; multiallelic sites are skipped.

docs.title: genoselect.read_plink(prefix)
docs.text:
Read a PLINK 1 binary fileset (.bed/.bim/.fam).

Dosage is the number of copies of the .bim A1 allele (0/1/2, NaN missing). Only SNP-major .bed files are supported (the PLINK default).

docs.title: genoselect.read_hapmap(path)
docs.text: Read a HapMap file into dosages = copies of the second listed allele.

docs.title: genoselect.GenotypeData dataclass
docs.text:
Genotypes plus identifiers.

geno is (n_samples, n_markers) allele dosage (0/1/2, NaN = missing).

docs.title: genoselect.simulate_population(n=200, m=1000, n_qtl=50, h2=0.5, seed=None)
docs.text:
Simulate n individuals genotyped at m biallelic markers.

n_qtl markers are causal with Normal effects; the genetic values are scaled to unit variance and Normal noise is added to give the target narrow-sense heritability h2.

docs.title: genoselect.qc_markers(geno, maf=0.05, max_missing=0.1, impute=True)
docs.text:
Filter markers by minor-allele frequency and missingness.

Returns (filtered_geno, keep_mask). With impute=True remaining missing values are mean-imputed.

docs.title: genoselect.impute_markers(geno)
docs.text: Mean-impute missing genotypes (NaN) column-wise (by 2p).

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
pip install genoselect

From source:

git clone https://github.com/mqfarooqi1/genoselect
cd genoselect && pip install -e ".[test]"

Requirements: Python 3.10+, NumPy, SciPy, scikit-learn, pandas.

docs.title: Data conventions
docs.text:
• Genotypes are a 2-D array of shape (n_individuals, n_markers), coded as allele dosage 0, 1, or 2.
  • Phenotypes are a 1-D array of length n_individuals.
  • Missing genotypes are NaN; impute them with impute_markers.

docs.title: Reading your data
docs.text:
All readers return a GenotypeData with .geno (n_samples × n_markers), .samples, and .markers, and add no dependencies.

import genoselect as gs

gd = gs.read_vcf("genotypes.vcf.gz")     # number of ALT alleles
gd = gs.read_plink("dataset")            # reads dataset.bed/.bim/.fam
gd = gs.read_hapmap("data.hmp.txt")      # HapMap text format

fit = gs.GBLUP().fit(gd.geno, phenotypes)

docs.title: GBLUP
docs.text:
fit = gs.GBLUP(min_maf=0.0).fit(geno, y)
fit.predict(geno_new)

Fitted by REML (Endelman 2011 spectral method). Useful attributes:

Attribute Meaning h2_ Estimated genomic heritability Vu_, Ve_, lambda_ Genetic / residual variance and ratio gebv_ Genomic estimated breeding values (training set) marker_effects_ RR-BLUP marker effects (for prediction)

A functional interface mirroring the R package is also available:

fit = gs.gblup(y, geno=geno)   # fitted GBLUP
sol = gs.gblup(y, K=G)         # REML solution dict

docs.title: Models and cross-validation
docs.text:
gs.available_models()
# ['gblup', 'elastic_net', 'random_forest', 'gradient_boosting', 'ensemble']

cv = gs.cross_validate(geno, y, models=["gblup", "elastic_net"],
                       k=5, scheme="kfold", random_state=0)
cv.summary()                   # model, mean, sd, n_folds

Accuracy is predictive ability — correlation between predicted and observed phenotypes on held-out folds. Use scheme="leave_group_out" with a groups array for forward-prediction scenarios. benchmark(...) runs all models.

docs.title: Stacked ensemble
docs.text:
ens = gs.StackedEnsemble(inner_k=5, random_state=0).fit(geno, y)
ens.weights_                   # non-negative, sum to 1
ens.predict(geno_new)

A super-learner combining the base models with NNLS weights from an inner cross-validation.

docs.title: GWAS
docs.text:
Single-marker association mapping with population-structure correction:

res = gs.gwas(geno, y, marker_map=mmap, n_pc=3)   # mmap has chrom/pos columns
gs.manhattan(res)      # position-aware Manhattan plot
gs.qq_plot(res)        # calibration check

gwas returns a DataFrame with per-marker effect, standard error, t, p and log10p. Plotting helpers require matplotlib (pip install genoselect[plot]).

docs.title: scikit-learn integration
docs.text:
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score
cross_val_score(gs.GBLUP(), geno, y, cv=5, scoring="r2")

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• VanRaden (2008), J. Dairy Sci. 91, 4414–4423. doi:10.3168/jds.2007-0980
  • Endelman (2011), Plant Genome 4, 250–255. doi:10.3835/plantgenome2011.08.0024
  • Meuwissen, Hayes & Goddard (2001), Genetics 157, 1819–1829.
  • van der Laan, Polley & Hubbard (2007), Stat. Appl. Genet. Mol. Biol. 6(1).

docs.title: genoselect — tutorial
docs.text:
In [1]: Copied! 
%matplotlib inline
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import genoselect as gs

# simulate a population: 250 individuals, 800 SNPs, 40 causal QTL, h2=0.5
pop = gs.simulate_population(n=250, m=800, n_qtl=40, h2=0.5, seed=1)
pop.geno.shape, pop.pheno.shape
%matplotlib inline import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import genoselect as gs # simulate a population: 250 individuals, 800 SNPs, 40 causal QTL, h2=0.5 pop = gs.simulate_population(n=250, m=800, n_qtl=40, h2=0.5, seed=1) pop.geno.shape, pop.pheno.shape Out[1]: 
((250, 800), (250,))
In [2]: Copied! 
fit = gs.GBLUP().fit(pop.geno, pop.pheno)
print(f'estimated genomic h2 = {fit.h2_:.3f}')
print(f'GEBV vs true breeding value corr = {np.corrcoef(fit.gebv_, pop.bv)[0,1]:.3f}')
fit = gs.GBLUP().fit(pop.geno, pop.pheno) print(f'estimated genomic h2 = {fit.h2_:.3f}') print(f'GEBV vs true breeding value corr = {np.corrcoef(fit.gebv_, pop.bv)[0,1]:.3f}') 
estimated genomic h2 = 0.394
GEBV vs true breeding value corr = 0.739
In [3]: Copied! 
cv = gs.cross_validate(pop.geno, pop.pheno,
        models=['gblup', 'elastic_net', 'random_forest', 'gradient_boosting'],
        k=5, random_state=1)
summary = cv.summary()
summary
cv = gs.cross_validate(pop.geno, pop.pheno, models=['gblup', 'elastic_net', 'random_forest', 'gradient_boosting'], k=5, random_state=1) summary = cv.summary() summary Out[3]: model mean sd n_folds 0 elastic_net 0.464567 0.069678 5 1 random_forest 0.374919 0.077158 5 2 gradient_boosting 0.273735 0.159740 5 3 gblup 0.183047 0.106422 5 In [4]: Copied! 
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 3.5))
ax.barh(summary['model'], summary['mean'], xerr=summary['sd'],
        color='#2a7f62', capsize=4)
ax.set_xlabel('cross-validated predictive ability')
ax.invert_yaxis(); ax.set_title('Model comparison'); fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 3.5)) ax.barh(summary['model'], summary['mean'], xerr=summary['sd'], color='#2a7f62', capsize=4) ax.set_xlabel('cross-validated predictive ability') ax.invert_yaxis(); ax.set_title('Model comparison'); fig.tight_layout() plt.show()

For this sparse trait (40 QTL of 800), variable-selection methods such as elastic net beat the infinitesimal GBLUP — a known result that the benchmark recovers automatically.

In [5]: Copied! 
tr, te = slice(0, 180), slice(180, 250)
ens = gs.StackedEnsemble(random_state=1).fit(pop.geno[tr], pop.pheno[tr])
print('weights:', {k: round(v, 3) for k, v in ens.weights_.items()})
pred = ens.predict(pop.geno[te])
print(f'ensemble pred vs true BV corr = {np.corrcoef(pred, pop.bv[te])[0,1]:.3f}')
tr, te = slice(0, 180), slice(180, 250) ens = gs.StackedEnsemble(random_state=1).fit(pop.geno[tr], pop.pheno[tr]) print('weights:', {k: round(v, 3) for k, v in ens.weights_.items()}) pred = ens.predict(pop.geno[te]) print(f'ensemble pred vs true BV corr = {np.corrcoef(pred, pop.bv[te])[0,1]:.3f}') 
weights: {'gblup': np.float64(0.0), 'elastic_net': np.float64(0.512), 'random_forest': np.float64(0.341), 'gradient_boosting': np.float64(0.146)}
ensemble pred vs true BV corr = 0.471

docs.title: genoselect — tutorial¶
docs.text:
scikit-learn-compatible genomic prediction in Python.

This tutorial fits GBLUP, benchmarks several genomic-prediction models by cross-validation, builds a stacked ensemble, and validates genoselect's GBLUP against the gold-standard R package rrBLUP.

docs.title: Install¶
docs.text: pip install genoselect

docs.title: Loading your own data¶
docs.text:
Instead of simulating, read VCF / PLINK / HapMap files in one line:

gd = gs.read_vcf('genotypes.vcf.gz')      # or gs.read_plink('data'), gs.read_hapmap('data.hmp.txt')
fit = gs.GBLUP().fit(gd.geno, phenotypes)

docs.title: GBLUP: heritability and breeding values¶
docs.text: GBLUP estimates variance components by REML and returns genomic estimated breeding values (GEBVs).

docs.title: Benchmark models by cross-validation¶
docs.text: All models share the scikit-learn fit/predict API. Accuracy is predictive ability — correlation between predicted and observed phenotypes on held-out folds.

docs.title: Stacked super-learner ensemble¶
docs.text: StackedEnsemble combines the base models with non-negative weights learned by inner cross-validation.

docs.title: Validation against rrBLUP¶
docs.text:
genoselect's GBLUP uses the same REML (Endelman, 2011) spectral method as the reference R package rrBLUP. Running both on identical data (200 individuals, 1000 markers) gives essentially identical results:

metric result genomic heritability h2 (genoselect / rrBLUP) 0.3538 / 0.3538 GEBV correlation (genoselect vs rrBLUP) 1.00000000 max absolute GEBV difference 6.7e-6 fit time (genoselect / rrBLUP) 0.04 s / 0.18 s

The two implementations agree to ~1e-6 (well below numerical noise), confirming genoselect is a faithful, correct GBLUP — and it runs faster.

Reproduce the rrBLUP side in R:

library(rrBLUP)
A   <- A.mat(geno - 1)          # geno coded 0/1/2
sol <- mixed.solve(y, K = A)    # sol$u = GEBVs; sol$Vu, sol$Ve

docs.title: Summary¶
docs.text:
• GBLUP — REML, validated against rrBLUP to ~1e-6
  • A common scikit-learn interface to GBLUP + ML models + a super-learner
  • Honest, breeding-relevant cross-validation
  • One-line readers for VCF / PLINK / HapMap

See the API reference and the GitHub repository.
